reasoning:
The word "chalaco" - referring to people from the port city of Callao, Peru - evokes harbor life: value arriving unpredictably on ships, dock workers showing up when work appears, tides that flow in and out, and the way harbors passively catch driftwood from every vessel that passes through.

Let me think about this problem through that lens...

Traditional retirement assumes income is a predictable stream you can divert. But for gig workers, seasonal workers, informal economy participants - income is more like ships arriving at a port: you don't know when, you don't know how much, you just show up and work when it's there.

The key insight from the harbor metaphor: **you can't track what flows in, but you can track what flows through.** Spending is visible and real, even when income is invisible and variable. Every transaction is a ship passing through the harbor.

What if retirement contributions happened at the point of *spending*, not earning? You don't save - the current saves for you.

```json
{
  "label": "Wake Drift Retirement",
  "design_principles": "Catch value as it flows through, don't try to divert a stream that doesn't exist. Design for people who can't plan. Make saving invisible and automatic.",
  "core_mechanism": "Transaction-based contributions: every time you spend, a percentage automatically drifts to retirement. You save by spending. The system catches the wake of your economic activity.",
  "how_it_works": "1) Every digital payment has a 'wake'—2-4% routed to your retirement account at point-of-sale. 2) Rate self-adjusts: if you're behind target trajectory, rate increases slightly; if ahead, decreases. 3) Contributions pool into a collectively-managed sovereign-style fund. 4) Individual shares tracked on ledger. 5) At retirement: perpetual income stream proportional to shares. 6) For low annual spenders, government matches contributions progressively to counter regressivity. 7) Cash transactions incentivized to digitize via small rebates.",
  "what_is_new": "Spending triggers saving, not income. Works for invisible/informal income because spending is always visible. Requires zero planning or willpower—the less attention you pay, the better it works.",
  "why_it_works": "Variable-income workers already adapt spending to income naturally. This piggybacks on that reality. Bad month = less spending = less contribution = no stress. Good month = more spending = more saved. The system breathes with your economic life instead of demanding consistency that doesn't exist.",
  "why_it_fails": "Regressive if unmitigated—poor people spend higher percentage of income on necessities. Requires massive payment infrastructure buy-in. Cash economies resist. People may resent invisible extraction even if beneficial. Rate self-adjustment could feel paternalistic or manipulative.",
  "medium_term": "Payment networks compete on 'wake efficiency.' New financial products emerge around spending-as-saving. Informal economy workers gain retirement visibility for first time. Cash usage declines further.",
  "long_term_vision": "Retirement anxiety disappears for variable-income workers. The distinction between 'saving' and 'living' dissolves—economic participation automatically builds future security. A generation retires who never once made a conscious retirement decision, yet arrives solvent. The harbor caught their driftwood all along."
}
```

solution.label: Wake Drift Retirement
solution.design_principles: Catch value as it flows through, don't try to divert a stream that doesn't exist. Design for people who can't plan. Make saving invisible and automatic.
solution.core_mechanism: Transaction-based contributions: every time you spend, a percentage automatically drifts to retirement. You save by spending. The system catches the wake of your economic activity.
solution.how_it_works: 1) Every digital payment has a 'wake'—2-4% routed to your retirement account at point-of-sale. 2) Rate self-adjusts: if you're behind target trajectory, rate increases slightly; if ahead, decreases. 3) Contributions pool into a collectively-managed sovereign-style fund. 4) Individual shares tracked on ledger. 5) At retirement: perpetual income stream proportional to shares. 6) For low annual spenders, government matches contributions progressively to counter regressivity. 7) Cash transactions incentivized to digitize via small rebates.
solution.what_is_new: Spending triggers saving, not income. Works for invisible/informal income because spending is always visible. Requires zero planning or willpower—the less attention you pay, the better it works.
solution.why_it_works: Variable-income workers already adapt spending to income naturally. This piggybacks on that reality. Bad month = less spending = less contribution = no stress. Good month = more spending = more saved. The system breathes with your economic life instead of demanding consistency that doesn't exist.
solution.why_it_fails: Regressive if unmitigated—poor people spend higher percentage of income on necessities. Requires massive payment infrastructure buy-in. Cash economies resist. People may resent invisible extraction even if beneficial. Rate self-adjustment could feel paternalistic or manipulative.
solution.medium_term: Payment networks compete on 'wake efficiency.' New financial products emerge around spending-as-saving. Informal economy workers gain retirement visibility for first time. Cash usage declines further.
solution.long_term_vision: Retirement anxiety disappears for variable-income workers. The distinction between 'saving' and 'living' dissolves—economic participation automatically builds future security. A generation retires who never once made a conscious retirement decision, yet arrives solvent. The harbor caught their driftwood all along.
